BEN: Just to end this out, I would say anyone who is looking to hire a copywriter… I don't care if it's me or anyone, it really doesn't matter… try to get with someone who understand audio, someone like Michael, for example, like you. If someone can do that, if they can just interview the person who created the product, maybe a couple of customers, you're going to add another layer to that pitch that you're writing that none of your competition is going to have, especially if it's a topic that is kind of deep in and of itself where it's kind of a complicated subject like that barter letter we did. Anyone who has a question may get it in the audio. So, I would definitely say find someone like Michael or anyone who knows how to do this sort of thing… and there's not many out there, unfortunately.

Get with them first before you go to the copywriter. See if you can't get some kind of an interview done, professional interview and the transcripts and maybe even some sort of an audio infomercial or something like that because it'll just make the copywriter's job that much more easy. I don't want to say easy, but it will definitely make the ad better.

MICHAEL: How can someone contact you by email if they had any questions?

---

Ben Settle is a marketing strategist who helps small companies and entrepreneurs get packed with new business.  For more information about his consulting and advertising services, contact him at www.BenSettle.com

# Crypto Copywriting Secrets

Introduction

Chapter 1: Everything You Ever Wanted to Know About Writing Headlines... But Were Afraid to Ask

Chapter 2: How to Nab More Smackeroos With A "Can't Ignore" Sales Letter Lead

Chapter 3: 6 Ways To Write Hot Sales Letter Stories That Bring In Lots of the Green Stuff (Even If You Screw Everything Else Up!)

Chapter 4: 12 Ways to Write Bullet Points That Give Prospects Almost NO CHOICE But to Buy From Your Sales Letters

Chapter 5: How To "Close the Deal" In Your Ads and Sales Letters

Afterword - Final Thoughts

---

# Introduction

If you want to double (even triple) the money you make from your sales letters... even if you're brand new to copywriting, and struggle with it now... then this short book will show you how.

It contains a very simple way of writing an ad or sales letter I've learned from over 10 years as an in-demand direct response copywriter working with some of the world's most successful 7-figure marketers and copywriters - like Ken McCarthy (million-dollar ad writer, and creator of "The System Seminar")... Mike Dillard (multi-million dollar info-marketer who had even consulted Robert Kiyosaki's website team)... Captain Chris Pizzo (#1 marketer in the hyper-competitive self-defense niche) and more.

I have helped my clients collectively earn millions of dollars in sales via my ads and sales letters. And this book contains the (very simple) way I use to write these ads, how I get "inside" the heads of my prospects and how YOU can now do the same.

One more thing before we begin.

This book is a compilation of newsletter issues (from the now out-of-print publication "The Crypto Marketing Newsletter") I wrote on the subject. It's structured as one big training, but it also has a lot of "side notes" in it (not part of the main teaching, but related to copywriting) that will help you become a better ad writer.

Enjoy the process of writing and making more money with your ads.

As the late (great) copywriter Gene Schwartz once said:

"Always shoot for the moon - it's one of the few real thrills left today."

-Ben Settle

http://www.BenSettle.com

---

# Chapter 1 Everything You Ever Wanted to Know About Writing Headlines... But Were Afraid to Ask

Dear Crypto Marketer,

Today's newsletter is the first in a series of 5 issues showing you, step by step, how to write profitable sales letters.

We'll primarily focus on sales letters selling info-products (i.e. books, DVD's, CD's, etc) as that's what the majority of subscribers sell. However, you can still apply all of it to selling anything else, too. For example, I use this same "formula" when writing ads selling videos just as easily as if I'm writing an ad selling a supplement. And the "secret formula" goes simply like this:

- Headline

- Lead

- Story

- Bullets

- Close

Nothing sexy or "world class" about it, is there? But methinks you'll find that my simple process of creating each of these components makes hammering out one profitable ad after another much, much easier - and maybe even "routine."

Sound cool?

Okay then, let's start with headlines. The first thing about writing headlines is... forget this whole idea of creating "killer" headlines. In fact, avoid writing headlines other copywriters and marketers consider "killer" or "cool" or any nonsense like that. What we want are headlines that get your sales letter read, not that get all the copywriting fanboys raving in the Internet marketing forums about how "killer" it is. And the first step of writing headlines that get your ad read is to make sure you are 1000% totally in "sync" with what your marketwants (and doesn't want).  So to that end, here's a list of questions to ask about your market before putting pen to paper:

- What keeps them AWAKE at night?

- What are they AFRAID of?

- What causes them PAIN?

- What HUMILIATES them?

- What makes them ANGRY?

- Who are they FURIOUS with?

- What do they WORRY about most?

- What are their VALUES like?

- What's their most URGENT, "hot button" crisis right now?

- Who are their ENEMIES?

- What INCOME bracket are they in?

- What are their POLITICAL views?

- What FRUSTRATES them most?

- What do they WANT more than anything?

- What RELIGION (if any) are they?

These questions get you thinking about your prospects and their problems, instead of your product and its features. And it goes back to the old adage:

"Don't tell me about your weed killer, tell me about my crab grass!"

In other words... talk about their problems, desires or wants, and you enter their "world." Once inside, you'll never bore them, lose their interest, or be ignored.

Okay, so that's step numero uno - get in sync with your market. Step two is the "what" to say.  Check this out: Once upon a time, one of the "old school" advertising agencies (I forget which one) invented what they called "knock-knocks" to write their headlines. And it works like this:  Imagine you are selling your product (whatever it is) door to door. No email, Internet or technology. And let's say you find a neighborhood where a lot of your prospects who have the problem your product solves live. So you walk up to one of the houses and you knock on the door. As soon as that door opens, what can you say, in one sentence, to the person who opens the door that:

1. Makes it impossible for them to slam the door on your face (like they might normally do to a sales rep)... and...

2. Gets them to literally invite you inside and beg you to tell them more?

In other words... what's the one thing you can say, to that prospect, at that exact moment in their life, with their unique problems, desires, fears and wants, that would make them practically jump for joy you showed up on their doorstep? For example, maybe you're writing to a bunch of guys who are getting older, are sort of "type A" weekend warriors who play hard, and have fantasies about being tough guys, kicking butt, and maybe even have regrets they didn't join the military or are starting to miss being in the military, etc. And because they are getting up there in age, they're starting to feel aches and pains, starting to worry about things like heart disease, cholesterol and "brain fog." They're also realizing they aren't the young, slim, "indestructible" men they were 10 years ago. In that case, you may use something like:

World's Leading Military Combat Instructor "De-classifies" The Secret Supplement Used By The Navy SEALS To Battle Pain, Injury, Body Fat And Cardiovascular Health Problems

See how that works?

Okay then, now that we know the "what" to say, it's time to learn "how" to say it. So what we're going to do now is, we're going to cover 10 different "fool proof" ways to write headlines. These are not the only ways to do it. But they are easy to use, and are surprisingly effective. Here goes...

1. The Proof Headline: Thank God for Gary Bencivenga. Before he started teaching his secrets publicly in his ezine (http://www.marketingbullets.com), most of us whipper-snapper copywriters had no idea how powerful using proof is. Take for example, the "Navy Seal" headline above. If it simply said, "How to Combat Pain, Injury, Body Fat and Cardiovascular Health Problems" it wouldn't have NEAR the impact it does by adding in the part about the client being the world's #1 military close combat instructor, and how the Navy Seals use his supplement. Like Gary B. says, join proof to your promise and response almost always goes up.

2. Market Oriented Headline: You hardly ever see a goo-roo use or even talk about these. But I can tell you this, headlines that address the market (and don't even necessarily make a claim) can explode your response. Example? Okay, here's a headline I wrote over a year and a half ago that's still going strong (even on high traffic news sites, like WorldNetDaily, Drudge, etc.):  WARNING: Do Not Read This If You Have Moral, Ethical Or Religious Reasons Against Hurting (Or Even Killing) Someone Who Violently Attacks You, Your Wife Or Your Kids.  No claim, benefit or anything "killer" about it, is there? It simply addresses the market.

3. The Emotion Headline:  This is where you tap into a strong emotion your prospect has. A "real life" example is this bad-boy that ran for over a year straight: "Warning:  Do Not Read This Unless You Are Frustrated As Hell With Your Golf Swing." Notice that "Warning" word again? And lookie - no claim or benefit!

4. The Offer Headline:  This is pretty straight forward. If you are writing to people who already want what you have, this can work like gangbusters. Example: "Free Fuel for a Year with Purchase of New Car - Today Only"

5. The Contrast Headline:  I love using contrast in my writing. Especially contrasting ideas. They immediately grab attention and they are very hard to ignore when properly targeted.  Examples: "Beat the Races by Picking Losers!" or "How to Lose Weight By Eating More of These Fatty Foods"

6. The Tabloid Headline: There's a reason tabloids get such high readership and sales: The headlines! Just add a sensational "twist" to your claims. Like, for example, John Carlton's now-famous "one legged golfer" ad: "Amazing Secret Discovered By One-Legged Golfer Adds 50 Yards to Your Drives, Eliminates Hooks and Slices… And Can Slash Up To 10 Strokes from Your Game Almost Overnight!"

7. The Curiosity Headline: I didn't realize just how powerful curiosity was until master copywriter Scott Haines (whose clients have included Jay Abraham and Donald Trump) said that was the #1 most powerful reason people buy.  Even more powerful than what people want! Here are some examples from legendary copywriter Mel Martin: "What Never to Eat on an Airplane" and "Bills It's Okay to Pay Late"

8. The Straight Benefit Headline: Hey, sometimes you can just say what you have to say with no messing around. Especially when talking about a hot problem people are really fired up about. Here's a classic Eugene Schwartz headline. Think this may get read if gas prices soar?  "Get Up to 12 Miles more Per Gallon from Your Car"

9. The Question Headline: I once heard it's neurologicallyimpossible for someone to ignore a question that's of interest to him or her. Is that really true? I don't know. But I do know question headlines work exceptionally well. Plus, there's no hypey feel to them and they don't look like "sales pitches." Here's a great example from copywriter Brian Keith Voiles: "Which Of These Health Problems Do You Want to Beat?"

10. The "Combine & Conquer" Headline: For best results, try to combine variations of the above. For example, combining a proof headline with a question headline ("When Doctors Have Headaches, What Do They Do?") or an emotion headline with a market-oriented headline ("An Open Letter To Readers of Black Belt Magazine Who Are Fed Up with the Hype Surrounding the Seemingly Endless String of 'Fighter-of-the-Month' Programs"), and so on and so forth.

These 10 formats are some of the best you can use. Just remember, you're always safe in your prospect's world. Write your headlines inside their world and you can never go wrong (no matter how you "dress" it up).

# # #

---

# Questions from The Crypto Market ing Mail Bag

QUESTION:  Ben, any tips on setting up joint ventures?  I have a product I want to sell to some big name lists but they don't even bother answering my emails!

Great question! Mayhaps these tips will help get your foot in a door or two...

- Sell to buyers:  Sales 101, my friend.  Seek people already doing a lot of JV's and affiliate offers in your market. They will be far more likely to hear you out and/or JV with you.  Frankly, they may even be waiting for you!

- Send a question, not your product: Never send your product to people unsolicited. I don't care how "killer" it is, either. Instead, ask them a question, respect their time and remove all pressure: "Do you do JV's with people who sell ____? I realize you have to be careful who you associate with, so I completely understand if the answer is no.  But I have a product that can help your list____.  If you're at all interested I would be happy to send it to you. There's no pressure, either way. And if you decide to pass on selling it, that's perfectly okay..."

- Don't be afraid to start small: You can also do reciprocal JV's with people of similar sized lists as yours. So if your list is 200 people, cross promote with someone else with a 200-person list (they'll be more likely to JV than someone with a list of, say, 20,000). If you're hell bent on JV'ing with a goo-roo, then try this: If you have 300 people on your list ask if they'd do an offer to 300 people on their list and you'll send an offer to your 300. This would also give you some potential social proof action for later (see below).

- Social proof: As you do more JV's, start using that "social proof" to your advantage. It's much easier to JV with Goo-roo Bob if you already JV'd with his best friends Goo-roo Joe or Newbie Nick (and vice versa). You're more "safe" to do business with when you have that connection.

# Crypto Copywriting Secrets in Response

Okay, Greg the good news is, we know there's a market for your product since it sells pretty well when you plug it in your articles on Lew Rockwell's site. (Note to reader: Greg also writes political articles on LewRockwell.com - good stuff, too).

So it's probably more a "positioning" thing than a copywriting thing on eBay. Here are some suggestions on how to squeeze more sales out of it:

- Position as Biz Opp: I'm not the expert on selling guns & ammo by any means. But I do know biz opps are hot right now - especially the worse the economy gets. And the beauty of this eBook is, you can use Obama to help you sell it! Most of us gun owners can't stand the anti-gun mafia. But at the same time, Obama is the most profitable thing that's ever happened to a lot of gun dealers, right? After all, sales for guns, ammo, etc. shot up in 2009 (while the crime rate decreased by 10% at the same time). So this is one of those products where you're really doing someone a service... and even a favor.  My suggestion is to write another version talking up how right now, and for at least the next 3 years, gun and ammo dealers are going to be making out like bandits (no pun intended).  And then talk about how this eBook is a step-by-step guide on how to start your own little "side business" selling guns & ammo. You can even show proof and examples of how, in some areas at least, guns and ammo are on backorder. That's the case in small towns like where I'm at. And I suspect you'd have quite a few people perking up when they realize they can make a nice side business selling guns & ammo online and sticking it to the gun-grabbing pinkos.

- Test Two Versions: Of course, you can also have a version up just for people already wanting to know how to sell their guns, like the one you have now. There's a lot of profit in market segmentation on eBay. A few years ago I joint ventured with Michael Senoff to sell a "how to buy businesses" course on eBay. We had pretty much the same ad up on 5 to 10 different auctions with slightly different headlines.  We had "How to Buy a Vending Machine Business" and "How Jewish People Can Buy a Business" and "How Real Estate Investors Can Buy a Business," etc. We got sales off most of these different segments. The more you can segment, the more you "up" your chances of sales.

- Hit Up Your Customers: Incidentally, you might want to ask your eBook customers who already bought what made them buy, too. What frustration were they trying to squash?  What were they searching for (keywords, etc.) when they found your auction? And anything else that may give you a lead on what your customers are responding to. It could be the real selling hot button is buried somewhere in the middle of the ad, and just needs to be moved to the top.

- Tell A Story Earlier: No matter which way you sell it, I would recommend starting the ad with a story your market can "step" into right away. For example, one story you could tell would be how people know your gun stance due to your articles on Lew Rockwell's site (and definitely drop names like that for affinity purposes). They were frustrated trying to sell at gun shows, had no time to do it, low on cash, etc. (all the hot buttons your market is feeling). So they started asking you for help to sell them on eBay knowing you are an eBay powerseller and specially trained by eBay, yada yada yada. Of course, you can't sell guns & ammo on eBay, so instead you showed them how to do it on other sites. Then, from there lead into the product.

- Higher Price: You might also try raising the price and seeing if that bumps sales and (even more importantly) profits. Often times a cheaper price can work almost as a "reverse" proof element - making people think, "How good can it be if it's only $10?"  So perhaps test everything from the current $9.95 all the way to $29, $39, $79, and even $99 (or more)?

- Headline: Finally, you may want to try a different sales letter headline. "Guns Galore" may not mean as much to someone as, "How to Buy & Sell Guns  and Ammo Legally in Online Auctions." Specificity often wins the day when it comes to headlines. And also, definitely play around with the headline ideas from this newsletter, too. You might just get a nice bump in sales...

Anyway, those are a few ideas I hope will help.

It's often harder to sell our own products than someone else's. And it always helps to have someone look at it like this, especially if sales are not where you want them to be.

Whew! Where did the time go?

Stay tuned for next month's issue. We're going to dive head first into how to write a sales letter "lead" (i.e. the opening paragraph/sentence - almost as important as the almighty headline.)

Laters,

Ben Settle
